title: Crème brûlée
subtitle: Baked vanilla custard with a caramelised sugar crust.
excerpt: A rich vanilla cream baked gently in a water bath until just set, chilled until cold, then finished at the last moment with a thin caramelised sugar crust. The definitive French dessert. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Crème brûlée. Baked custard with a sugar crust appears in French culinary literature from the 17th century. The dish was popularised in Britain at Trinity College, Cambridge ('Trinity burnt cream'). The modern version with a blowtorch-caramelised crust became a restaurant standard from the 1980s onwards, documented in texts by Escoffier and in modern French pastry manuals.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The custards can be baked and chilled up to 2 days ahead. Add the sugar and caramelise immediately before serving.

content.text: The custard must not be over-baked. Take it from the oven while the centre still has a visible wobble; it sets completely as it chills. A fully set custard straight from the oven will be grainy and over-cooked after chilling. The water bath is not optional: it insulates the custard from the direct heat and produces the smooth, trembling texture that defines the dish.

items.prepNote: split and scraped; or 2 tsp vanilla extract

content.text: Heat {{double-cream}} of cream with {{vanilla-pod}} vanilla pod and seeds until steaming. Whisk {{eggs}} egg yolks with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar until combined. Pour the hot cream onto the yolks slowly, whisking constantly. Strain into a jug and pour into four ramekins. Set the ramekins in a roasting tin and pour boiling water to come halfway up their sides. Bake at 150°C for 30 to 35 minutes until the edges are set and the centre wobbles like jelly. Cool, then chill for at least 4 hours.

Sprinkle a thin, even layer of caster sugar over each custard. Use a blowtorch to caramelise the sugar to a deep amber, moving the flame constantly to avoid scorching. Serve within 5 minutes before the crust softens.

items.cause: Over-baked, or cream added too hot to the yolks too quickly.
items.fix: Pour the cream slowly and whisk continuously. Remove from the oven while the centre still wobbles.

items.symptom: Sugar crust too thick and hard
items.fix: A thin, even dusting is enough. One tablespoon of sugar per ramekin; tap the ramekin to level it.
Use boiling water from a kettle. Check the oven temperature with a thermometer if the custard is still liquid after 4 hours of chilling.

content.text: Crème brûlée is the dessert that non-cooking restaurant guests order and home cooks are intimidated by. The technique is not difficult, but it requires precision and timing. A blowtorch is the right tool; the grill produces an uneven result. Made and chilled in advance, it is one of the most reliable dinner-party endings there is.
